title: Wire Weld Penetration and Common Defect Solutions
summary: This section covers troubleshooting weld quality issues for MIG and flux-cored wire welding, including excess penetration, burn-through, inadequate penetration, weld not adhering, and slag formation. It provides possible causes and solutions for each defect type. Visual profile views accompany each defect description.
key_facts: Excess penetration or burn-through can be caused by workpiece overheating or travel speed too slow.
Reducing wire feed speed and decreasing weld current addresses workpiece overheating.
Inadequate penetration can result from incorrect welding technique or insufficient weld heat.
Maintain 1/2 inch or less contact-tip-to-work distance (CTWD) for proper penetration.
Keep arc on the leading edge of the weld puddle for proper penetration.
Thick workpieces should be beveled, allowed a slight gap, and welded in several passes.
Slag is a necessary part of a flux-cored wire weld and shields the weld from impurities.
Clean off slag with a chipping hammer and wire brush after welding.
Gas-shielded MIG welds are protected by shielding gas and do not need slag.
Weld not adhering can be caused by incorrect technique, insufficient heat, dirty workpiece, or narrow gap.
Clean workpiece down to bare metal to address weld adhesion issues from a dirty workpiece.
Pausing briefly at sides during weave bead helps prevent weld adhesion problems.
warnings: Make tack welds to hold workpieces.
Weld a small portion and allow to cool before proceeding.
query_terms: CTWD contact tip to work distance
